slides.title: Hackathon Report: Separating Verifier and Relying Party Responsibilities in TEEP
Verifiable Proof of Environment Attestation Profile (V-PEA)

polls.text: how many people have read the draft
What is the interest in adopting?

chat.author: Jean-Pierre Fiset
chat.text: New slides were uploaded fro "Evidence Encoding for HSMs".

chat.author: Jean-Pierre Fiset
chat.text: Would it be possible to approve them?

chat.author: Thomas Fossati
chat.text: yes

chat.text: sounds fine remotely

chat.author: Nathanael Ritz
chat.text: Names are _____

chat.author: Carl Wallace
chat.text: Some RFC = "Cryptographic Message Syntax"

chat.author: Thomas Fossati
chat.text: :rolling_on_the_floor_laughing:

chat.author: Carl Wallace
chat.text: [mic] Why TBS-like instead of SignedData reuse? This was part of the SignerIdentifier comment.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: Section 3, right?

chat.author: Carl Wallace
chat.text: My question is relative to the data model in Section 5

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: Carl - I'll ask

chat.author: Carl Wallace
chat.text: it's already on the list, so just answer there

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: Carl, please send the question to the list and the authors will address it.

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: Thank you!

chat.author: Carl Wallace
chat.text: (not a renaming question)

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Carl Wallace said:

My question is relative to the data model in Section 5

My comment was for J-P's comment to my question; not yours.

chat.author: Christopher Inacio
chat.text: remote folks: Can you hear?

chat.author: Nathanael Ritz
chat.text: Yes

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text:
Carl Wallace said:

[mic] Why TBS-like instead of SignedData reuse? This was part of the SignerIdentifier comment.

Are you suggesting
import CryptographicMessageSyntax2004 (RFC5652)


instead of defining new structures here?
If so, given this WG's general umm, affinity level for ASN.1, we decided early in the design to define new CMS-like structures that remove a lot of the complexity of CMS rather than importing the entire bag of def's and structs, to only use a small corner of them.

chat.author: Carl Wallace
chat.text: This is not really CMS-like but is the even older "to be signed" model. Reinventing stuff like certs bags, counter signatures, etc. that CMS already does, does not strike me as much of a win. Including a few words re: why not CMS would be helpful. For the RATS folks, this is analogous to rolling your own COSE.

chat.author: Jean-Pierre Fiset
chat.text: One of the use case requires the HSM to parse the structure. Therefore, we wanted to keep the structure simple.

chat.author: Jean-Pierre Fiset
chat.text: HSMs are capable of parsing certificate-like structure. CMS is much larger to swallow.

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: Yogesh has been assigned as shepherd in the datatracker - thank you!

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text: The simple answer is that if we started from CMS, then we could be in for a big exercise of forbidding the use of 90% of CMS, which to me seems like a much bigger exercise than inventing a couple new simple structs.

chat.author: Carl Wallace
chat.text: There are CMS-based cert req protocols, so SignedData is well worn ground for CAs

chat.author: Carl Wallace
chat.text: i will complete my review of the draft with this orientation in mind. when i stopped before, i was not finding the things like counter signatures any simpler.

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text:
Carl Wallace said:

There are CMS-based cert req protocols, so SignedData is well worn ground for CAs

Sure, but that's not the direction we're worried about.
The direction we're worried about is when an HSM has to _parse_ one of these evedince formats, and you don't want an HSM doing a big complicated CMS parse _inside the trust boundary_. Especially if the HSM doesn't support CMS today. Simpler structs are a win there.

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text:
Example of why an HSM would need to _parse_ a KeyEvidence:

You are transferring a key from one HSM to another, and parsing the KeyEvidence is part of the import.
Clustering HSMs could involve the HSMs attesting to each other that they all have a consistent state for a given replicated key,
etc.

chat.author: Carl Wallace
chat.text: i understand why one would need key evidence. i don't (yet) understand why we would want to invent a signing structure (especially one that uses cert bags and countersignatures) instead of using the one we have.

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text: At some point during the development of this, we took a survey of nShield, Crypto4A, Thales, Utimaco on the question: "Who already has a CMS parser inside your HSM kernel? Do you want to add one?". If I recall correctly, the answer was a resounding No.

chat.author: Carl Wallace
chat.text: perhaps a question now is "how far are we from CMS given the inclusion of cert bags and counter signatures."

chat.author: Carl Wallace
chat.text: (i think this is exhausting the zulip medium and is better moved to the list)

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: For coserv authors: https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-sardar-rats-sec-cons-04.html#section-8.2

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: I haven't seen this being addressed or acknowledged in the draft.

chat.author: Ned Smith
chat.text: note: there is at least one rats draft focused on transforming evidence

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: Usama, the authors responded. I will step in to help close this out, but it will be following this week as I have a busy week and weekend of meetings.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar

chat.author: Nathanael Ritz
chat.text: 600+ issues…

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: Chairs will assess and it should not require to be stated a 3rd time.

chat.author: Nathanael Ritz
chat.text: 600+ issues…

chat.author: Nathanael Ritz
chat.text: Mobile sucks. Anyway, just impressed with the amount of work done

chat.author: Paul Howard
chat.text: Agree. Brilliant work on CoRIM.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Kathleen Moriarty said:

Chairs will assess and it should not require to be stated a 3rd time.

Thanks. That's exactly my point. It is frustrating to repeat the same concerns each time. They are repeated because they are unaddressed.

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: Usama, please stop.

chat.author: Michael Richardson
chat.text:
Carl Wallace said:

perhaps a question now is "how far are we from CMS given the inclusion of cert bags and counter signatures."

As a survivor of CMS, in RFC8366, we used hardly any of CMS in the end.  We were pushed toward it because the code was already present in the already-FIPS reviewed libraries (really, just libssl.so.1.x.y) in our target platforms.    The small saving in writing ASN.1 might be interesting from a document writer point of view, from an implementer point of view, there are just so many things to get wrong (i.e. underspecified) when using CMS...  We'd need to have an entire section that lists CMS things NOT to do.

chat.author: Nathanael Ritz
chat.text: I’m volunteering too

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: Thank you!

chat.author: Carl Wallace
chat.text: Most of CMS simply does not apply (encryption, MACs, digested data, etc), so I don't find this particularly persuasive. But we're just covering the same ground. Consider me "not yet sold" that reinventing cert bags and counter signatures is a good idea.

chat.author: Christopher Inacio
chat.text:
Kathleen Moriarty said:

Chairs will assess and it should not require to be stated a 3rd time.

What the process requires is that your technical concerns are addressed, not necessarily resolved.

chat.author: Nathanael Ritz
chat.text: Which bof?

chat.author: Thomas Fossati
chat.text: DAWN

chat.author: Nathanael Ritz
chat.text: Ah, that’s a very… active mailing list right now

chat.author: Paul Howard
chat.text: +1 to Yogesh's suggestion of looking at discovery within RATS as a whole. CoSERV recognises similar challenges (Appendix C), but has only illustrative solutions, not normative ones.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
With nonce and public key as binding, relay attacks apply: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/408219182_Intra-handshakefail_CVE-2026-33697_High-severity_CVE_in_Attested_TLS
Proof here: https://github.com/muhammad-usama-sardar/intra-handshake.fail

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: https://www.cve.org/CVERecord?id=CVE-2026-33697

chat.author: Ken Takayama
chat.text: Thanks, Usama!

chat.author: Paul Howard
chat.text: To Ned's point about CoSERV being a discovery mechanism, that's true in the sense that it offers an API for querying artifacts. But it pre-supposes that the consumer has discovered the provider (e.g. its base URL). The bootstrapping phase of discovery is something that CoSERV could potentially benefit from, if there was a RATS-wide solution (or some suitable generic solution).

chat.author: Ned Smith
chat.text: @Paul - Discovery can be a multi-stage problem. DNS for hosts CoSERV for deeper content

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Ken Takayama said:

Thanks, Usama!

Welcome. Could you share link to your implementation for notes?

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: I don't see it in your slides.

chat.author: Michael Richardson
chat.text:
Carl Wallace said:

Most of CMS simply does not apply (encryption, MACs, digested data, etc), so I don't find this particularly persuasive. But we're just covering the same ground. Consider me "not yet sold" that reinventing cert bags and counter signatures is a good idea.

Yes, exactly. MOST of it does not apply.  Something has to tell implementers about all the things they MUST NOT use. That's not impossible to do, but in effect, we are showing developers (who didn't understand the motivations of the spec), a bunch of nice levers that they shouldn't use.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: Carl, mcr, Mike: can one of you summarize this whole discussion in notes, please? It is hard to follow for me.

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: The discussion can go to the list so it can continue there.

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: Sure, I think it would be helpful to summarize in notes, too.

chat.author: Carl Wallace
chat.text: Not only is it not impossible to do, it need not be done. What difference does it make that CMS defines a EnvelopedData and you aren't using it here? To flip your "document writer" comment around, how many people have running CMS code today and how many have running code for this "simpler" TBS struct with certs and counter signatures? Honestly, it was the counter signature notion that pushed me past thinking using TBS is OK here.

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text: I did already put a summary in the minutes.

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: Thank you, Mike!

chat.author: Carl Wallace
chat.text: (i will respond no further on this here, Kathleen, sorry this has dragged on here)

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: Not a problem! It looks like it was useful.

chat.author: Ken Takayama
chat.text:
Usama,
Our project portal is available here:
https://github.com/s-miyazawa/teep-wasm-demo
I believe our TAM implementation is the key component in determining whether this CVE can be exploited:
https://github.com/kentakayama/AttesTAM#attestam

This AttesTAM implementation makes a stricter choice: it issues a fresh, one-time nonce as a challenge, requires the attestation Evidence to be bound to that exact value, and rejects replayed or reused Evidence.

Therefore, I believe this implementation prevents the attack described in the CVE from being successfully carried out.

chat.author: Mike Ounsworth
chat.text:
@Carl Wallace So, the authors group of this draft is far more interested in the implementability of this spec within HSMs than within CA / RA stacks since the former tends to have much tighter code review, code space, and security requirements.
From a survey of the participating HSM vendors, the answer to both:
"Who currently implements CMS"
and
"Who currently implements this not-yet-defined-thing"
was the same: zero.

chat.author: Nathanael Ritz
chat.text: @Ken you need to capture session material associated with the transport. If this is not over a network transport then that might be N/A

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Ken Takayama said:

Usama,
Our project portal is available here:
https://github.com/s-miyazawa/teep-wasm-demo
I believe our TAM implementation is the key component in determining whether this CVE can be exploited:
https://github.com/kentakayama/AttesTAM#attestam

This AttesTAM implementation makes a stricter choice: it issues a fresh, one-time nonce as a challenge, requires the attestation Evidence to be bound to that exact value, and rejects replayed or reused Evidence.

Therefore, I believe this implementation prevents the attack described in the CVE from being successfully carried out.

Thanks, added the links. I don't see how the attack is avoided. Please see Sec. 7.1.6 of https://www.researchgate.net/publication/408219182_Intra-handshakefail_CVE-2026-33697_High-severity_CVE_in_Attested_TLS for attack on nonce and public key binder.

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: A transformation

chat.author: Carl Wallace
chat.text:
Mike Ounsworth said:

Carl Wallace So, the authors group of this draft is far more interested in the implementability of this spec within HSMs than within CA / RA stacks since the former tends to have much tighter code review, code space, and security requirements.
From a survey of the participating HSM vendors, the answer to both:
"Who currently implements CMS"
and
"Who currently implements this not-yet-defined-thing"
was the same: zero.

Since this is continuing....sure, and seeking to standardize something begs different questions from a larger community.

chat.author: Ken Takayama
chat.text:
Thanks, added the links. I don't see how the attack is avoided. Please see Sec. 7.1.6 for attack on nonce and public key binder.

We compute the SHA-384 hash of:
32-byte x-coordinate || 32-byte y-coordinate || TAM challenge
and place the resulting digest in the SGX Quote’s Report Data field. The x- and y-coordinates represent the public key of the TEEP Agent running inside a particular Enclave instance. This public key is used by the TEEP Agent to sign TEEP messages.
Each challenge is globally unique across all TEEP Agents and is never reused. After the corresponding Quote has been successfully verified, the challenge is marked as used, and any subsequent Evidence containing the same challenge is rejected.

chat.text: (silly note: I’ve just moved to a village whose inhabitants are called "les rats", which is French for RATS! I feel right at home!)

chat.text: draft-lkspa-rats-verifiable-geo-fence section 6.2.2 on GNSS Spoofing discusse my point

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text:
Ken Takayama said:


Thanks, added the links. I don't see how the attack is avoided. Please see Sec. 7.1.6 for attack on nonce and public key binder.

We compute the SHA-384 hash of:
32-byte x-coordinate || 32-byte y-coordinate || TAM challenge
and place the resulting digest in the SGX Quote’s Report Data field. The x- and y-coordinates represent the public key of the TEEP Agent running inside a particular Enclave instance. This public key is used by the TEEP Agent to sign TEEP messages.
Each challenge is globally unique across all TEEP Agents and is never reused. After the corresponding Quote has been successfully verified, the challenge is marked as used, and any subsequent Evidence containing the same challenge is rejected.

Whether you have nonce || public key as in paper or public key || nonce as you have, they are logically the same; the attack still applies.

chat.text: Chairs, if there is time, you could project https://github.com/mcr/wiki.ietf.org/blob/rats-wg/group/rats/atomic-composites.md for comment.

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: It was presented, there was one question that was not addressed and would be taken to the list. The work will be divided up between several working groups, (e.g. NTP, RATS). Adoption call would follow an assessment of interest. If we are able to clear out the drafts that said they were ready to be progressed, then this should be ok to do an adoption call. This is more complex then the other draft concept where translation/transformation between formats.

chat.text: Paul, I just saw: https://github.com/mcr/wiki.ietf.org/tree/rats-wg/rats  and probably it belongs in group/rats ?

chat.author: Giridhar Mandyam
chat.text: 9711 encryption of evidence:  EAT makes no assumptions about transport of attestation.  I did try to put in the role of transport security in the SecCons section.

chat.author: Kathleen Moriarty
chat.text: Each person and response has 1 minute

chat.author: Michael Richardson
chat.text: My take is that prior to 9334's split of Verifier/RP, that the RP (DIsney+ for instance), was ALWAYS malicious and coercise, and it thus sites/applications REGULARLY forced me to configure my devices in a particular way... OR ELSE.   After 9334, we have drawn a tighter circle of trust around the Verifier, and perhaps.... created an opportunity for the Verifier to be other than a monopoly/cartel.

chat.author: Giridhar Mandyam
chat.text: Verifiers can collude to build up richer information about the attester.   Verifier A could be authorized to only receive claim set 1, Verifier B for claim set 2.  But the composite of claim sets 1 and 2 can become available to both verifiers.

chat.author: Ken Takayama
chat.text:
Muhammad Usama Sardar said:

Ken Takayama said:


Thanks, added the links. I don't see how the attack is avoided. Please see Sec. 7.1.6 for attack on nonce and public key binder.

We compute the SHA-384 hash of:
32-byte x-coordinate || 32-byte y-coordinate || TAM challenge
and place the resulting digest in the SGX Quote’s Report Data field. The x- and y-coordinates represent the public key of the TEEP Agent running inside a particular Enclave instance. This public key is used by the TEEP Agent to sign TEEP messages.
Each challenge is globally unique across all TEEP Agents and is never reused. After the corresponding Quote has been successfully verified, the challenge is marked as used, and any subsequent Evidence containing the same challenge is rejected.

Whether you have nonce || public key as in paper or public key || nonce as you have, they are logically the same; the attack still applies.

I may have misunderstood some aspects of this CVE. I would appreciate some time to study it more carefully and reassess whether our implementation is exposed to the vulnerability or any related risks.
Once I have a better understanding, would it be okay for me to continue the discussion on the mailing list? Thank you in advance for your patience.

chat.author: Michael Richardson
chat.text:
Michael Richardson said:

My take is that prior to 9334's split of Verifier/RP, that the RP (DIsney+ for instance), was ALWAYS malicious and coercise, and it thus sites/applications REGULARLY forced me to configure my devices in a particular way... OR ELSE.   After 9334, we have drawn a tighter circle of trust around the Verifier, and perhaps.... created an opportunity for the Verifier to be other than a monopoly/cartel.

(But, not every Verifiers will be trusted by every RP, so the RP still can coercer an Attester to work with a particular kind/brand/... of Verifier.  So, the conflict is between Verifier/RP now, rather than Attester/RP)

I may have misunderstood some aspects of this CVE. I would appreciate some time to study it more carefully and reassess whether our implementation is exposed to the vulnerability or any related risks.
